focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The records alcove at Caldera Junction sat above the sanitation bay, and everything in it sweated by association. The floor was dry, the cabinets were dry, the forms were dry, but the heat from the wash line climbed through the metal anyway and turned the room into a place where paper curled at the corners before noon. Marisol let herself in with her passcard, felt the old catch in her left knee on the threshold, and locked the door behind her. Through the wall she could hear the blackwater pump on Car 6 taking its load in slow surges, a wet-choked thump followed by the cleaner whine of the recovery line. It was southbound prep. She had two hours, maybe less, before dispatch wanted every signature in place and every body counted as movable.

She set Gabriel's case number on the blotter. Numbers behaved better when you pinned them down. 4471-R. The terminal woke under her hand with a patch of dead pixels in one corner and the yellow RailPassage logo opening like bad milk. She logged into route review, then into treaty mirror, then into the secondary custody screen that still ran on old government architecture and always lagged when the corridor got busy. The train below her vibrated through the floor in a steady floorboard hum. Somebody dropped a wrench in the bay and swore. A synthetic announcement repeated boarding restrictions in English and Spanish, polite as a church volunteer and just as deaf.

She did not need hope. She needed a sentence. Somewhere in all the forms there had to be one sentence that let a living woman tell the machine it had the wrong country.

The file opened on the fourth try.

RAFAEL AVELAR.

CASE 4471-R.

RECEIVING STATE: SANTA AVELINA.

ROUTING BASIS: A-3 PARENTAL REGISTRY.

PRE-TRANSFER REVIEW: UNAVAILABLE WHILE ANCESTRAL CLAIM I...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...would settle into sense. He rehooked the short chain, took the property bag, and tugged Gabriel to his feet. Gabriel stood carefully, protecting the right side now that the wrap had made the pain more legible instead of less.

At the threshold he stopped and turned back just enough to make Marisol meet his eyes one last time.

"The guards leave bruises," he said. "You built the part that lets everybody else call them manageable."

Baca jerked the chain. Gabriel did not look at her again.

Celia stripped the paper backing from another wrap with her teeth. "Next time," she said without malice, tired almost to transparency, "bring me a body you actually mean to keep."

Then she was gone behind the curtain and the room reclaimed itself: oxygen hiss, cart wheel squeak, fluorescent buzz, the floor drain swallowing diluted blood one pink mouthful at a time.

Marisol stood alone beside the empty stainless cart until the intake speaker outside announced final southbound staging. Her passcard trace was in the system now. Baca had seen too much urgency. Celia had seen more than that. Gabriel had refused the one version of mercy she knew how to offer and named the job beneath the job with a precision she could not scrub down into clerical language.

When she stepped back into the corridor, the heat hit her full in the face. At the far end Baca turned Gabriel toward the loading gate, the property bag with the recorder knocking lightly against his knee as if it held nothing heavier than laces and a broken pen. Marisol watched them disappear into the white-painted passage where rust kept showing through no matter how often the crews came at it with bleach, and the sentence she had been using to keep herself functional since dawn finally failed.

This was not a mistake inside the line.
